questions.question: Dogfooding: do we apply the new judge-panel rule to this feature's own FF workflow once the runner changes land mid-feature, or treat the whole thing as Level 1 voluntary and apply Level 2 only to future features?
questions.recommendation: Treat the whole build as Level 1 throughout. Apply Level 2 to future features only, starting with retroactively unblocking orchestrator + Finding #2 after this ships.
questions.rationale: Mid-feature rule switching is operationally messy — state.json format would need migration, and partially applying a half-built runner to its own feature invites foot-guns. Cleaner: ship the feature end-to-end with discipline (I strictly apply the convergence rule manually in my role), then first real test is unblocking the two waiting features.

questions.question: Back-test scope: build the quarterly back-test loop (correlate unresolved_concerns with actual regressions) as part of this feature, or ship judge-panel enforcement first and add back-test as a follow-up?
questions.recommendation: Follow-up feature. Scope this one to just the enforcement mechanism.
questions.rationale: Back-test needs real shipped data to be meaningful (requires features to have already flowed through the new rule for weeks). Building it now means we're writing a script to analyze an empty corpus. Ship enforcement, accumulate ~10-15 features of data, then build the back-test as its own feature with real signal. Smaller blast radius per PR. Also avoids scope bloat on this feature.

questions.question: Judge prompts: do they ship as fixed templates in the repo (versioned with code) or as configurable files operators can tweak per-workflow without a code change?
questions.recommendation: Fixed templates in the repo, versioned with the runner. One file per judge under docs/workflow/operations/codex-skills/feature-factory/judge-prompts/.
questions.rationale: Per-workflow tweakability sounds flexible but invites (a) drift between features, (b) gaming ('let's loosen the Completeness prompt for this feature'), and (c) a back-test nightmare because we can't compare votes if prompts differ. Fixed+versioned means we change the prompts via a PR (tracked, reviewable, reasoned). If a prompt is wrong we fix it once for everyone. Operators can still override via env vars or CLI flags in edge cases, but the default path is frozen templates.

discovery.summary: Runner-enforced judge panel for FF: after 3 adversarial rounds per stage, dispatch 3 judges (Codex mini + Codex heavy + Claude Sonnet 4.6) with distinct lenses. Majority 2/3 proceed advances; 2/3 block triggers edit + re-vote (max 3 judge rounds). Unresolved concerns get injected into PR. Three shaping decisions: dogfooding, back-test scope, judge-prompt flexibility.

answers.Dogfooding: do we apply the new judge-pa: Level 1 throughout this build. Orchestrator strictly applies the 3-round convergence cap manually. Level 2 ships, then first real use is retroactively unblocking orchestrator + Finding #2.
answers.Back-test scope: build the quarterly bac: Both together. Feature includes: (a) enforcement runner changes, (b) back-test script that correlates unresolved_concerns with CI regressions + PR reverts + prod incidents, runnable as a cron/manual script. Back-test starts returning empty results at launch; will produce real signal after ~10-15 features have flowed through the new rule.
answers.Judge prompts: do they ship as fixed tem: Fixed templates in repo under docs/workflow/operations/codex-skills/feature-factory/judge-prompts/. CLI flag --prompt-override path/to/custom.md as escape hatch, logged to state.json. Specific prompt content TBD pending parallel research agents.
